caption: Figure 1. XRD patterns of the a) 0.02Nb steels and b) 0.04Nb steels, at different aging states.
paper_abstract: Microalloying of Nb has been commonly utilized to enhance the structure and mechanical properties of advanced high-strength steel (AHSS), but its application in medium-Mn low-density steel has been relatively understudied. This study aims to investigate the evolution of nano-scale precipitates and mechanical properties of Fe-12Mn-9Al-3Cr-1.4C-0.02/0.04Nb low-density steels. The findings reveal that the austenite grain size of 0.04Nb steels is approximately half that of 0.02Nb steels due to the precipitation of NbC particles. Moreover, the addition of Nb is found to elevate the formation energy of κ-carbide, thereby impeding its growth and coarsening. Consequently, the intragranular κ-carbides in 0.04Nb steels are consistently smaller than those in 0.02Nb steels, with no coarsened intergranular κ-carbides detected in either steel variant after aging treatment at 723–823 K. Despite a slight reduction on precipitation strengthening of κ-carbides with higher Nb content, the yield strength of 0.04Nb steels exceeds that of 0.02Nb steels by ≈71 MPa, mainly due to additional fine grain strengthening and dislocation strengthening. The strengthening mechanisms in the Nb-containing low-density steels are analyzed quantitatively. The role of Nb in regulating the microstructure and mechanical properties of medium-Mn low-density steels is comprehensively discussed, offering valuable insights for the alloy design of lowdensity steel.
paper_title: Microstructural Evolution and Mechanical Properties of Nb-Containing Medium-Mn Low-Density Steels
citation_sentences: displays the XRD patterns of the 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb Figure 1steels, in which the microstructure of all the Nb-containing steels consists of ferrite and austenite. After aging treatments at different temperatures, the diffraction peak intensity of both austenite and ferrite in the 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels shows almost no significant change, as depicted in Figure 1a,b.

citation_sentences: shows SEM images of the 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels Figure 2aged at different temperatures. It can be found that irregularshaped ferrite is randomly dispersed within the austenite matrix. In order to investigate the effects of Nb content and aging treatment temperature on the microstructure of low-density steels, an ImageJ software was employed to measure the ferrite content in different samples. During the measurement process, an image selection tool in the software was used to select all irregularly shaped ferrite in SEM images, and calculated the proportion of ferrite phase area in the SEM images as the ferrite content. To ensure the accuracy of statistical data, more than five SEM images were selected for each sample for analysis, and the average value of the measurement results was taken as the ferrite content. The ferrite content in the as-cast 0.02Nb steel is measured as ≈16.2 vol%. Following aging treatment at 723, 773, and 823 K for 3 h, the ferrite content in the steels is 16.4, 17.1, and 18.3 vol%, respectively. Similarly, the 0.04Nb steels under the same aging states have ferrite contents of 16.3, 17.2, 18.4, and 18.1 vol%, respectively. These results indicate that the ferrite contents in the low-density steels are not affected by the aging temperature, which aligns with the observation from XRD analysis. Furthermore, the Nb addition leads to the formation of submicron white particles within the austenite matrix, as indicated by the purple arrows in Figure 2.  shows the Figure 3TEM selected area electron diffraction (SAED) analysis and corresponding EDS analysis of the white particles in as-cast 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels, in which the white particles are identified as NbC. In order to analyze the evolution of NbC particles in 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels, at least five SEM images of each sample were selected for analysis by ImageJ software. According to the statistical results shown in , both the size and volume Figure 4fraction of the NbC particles increase slightly with higher aging temperature and more Nb content.

shows EBSD orientation color maps for the Figure 5Nb-containing steels. Although both ferrite and austenite were present in the EBSD maps, these two phases could be clearly distinguished based on the morphology of ferrite in Figure 2.
